title: Coil-built planter in air-dry clay
subtitle: A tall, textured planter for indoor cacti and succulents
excerpt: A coil-built planter is a good second project after the coil-pot bowl: it is taller, needs drainage planning, and benefits from a textured exterior that the coil ridges naturally provide. Air-dry clay is not waterproof, so this planter is designed to hold a terracotta liner with the real plant inside. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Coil-building technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: -built planter is designed to hold a smaller terracotta pot inside it, keeping the plant's roots in proper drainage while the exterior is a hand-built clay shell. Air-dry clay softens when wet, so the liner is needed. Build the planter to hold a 10-cm terracotta pot comfortably. No kiln or wheel is needed, but the build takes two days.

attrs.body: Measure the terracotta pot before starting. The planter's interior diameter should be 2-3 cm wider than the outer diameter of the liner so it drops in and out without force.
attrs.title: Using a liner

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 10 mm guide rails

content.text: Roll a slab 10 mm thick. Cut a circle 16 cm in diameter: this gives the interior the space needed for the liner with room around it. Compact the slab with a wooden rib from the centre outward.
Step 2: Add the first three coil layers

Roll coils 15 mm in diameter. Score the perimeter of the base, apply slurry, and press the first coil around the edge. Blend the interior seam. Add two more coils in the same way, blending each interior seam before the next. Leave the exterior coil ridges visible for texture. Cover with plastic and leave overnight.

, the lower walls now support more coils safely. Add three to four more coil layers, blending the interior of each before moving to the next. For a 12-cm tall planter, around six or seven coils are needed in total.
Step 4: Shape and finish the rim

Trim the top coil level with the needle tool and compress the rim with a wet finger to round and seal the edge. Leave the exterior ridged or smooth it with a damp sponge for a different effect. Dry bone-dry over two to three days. Seal with PVA inside and out, paying particular attention to the inside base.
Press leaves into the clay between the coils. The leaves leave a print on the outside.
Use paper clay for a planter taller than 15 cm: the fibre improves join strength under the weight of the upper walls.

attrs.intro: Problems in building a taller coil pot.

items.fix: Allow the lower walls to reach leather-hard before continuing. Use a wooden rib pressed against the outside of the wall to push bulges back in while the clay is still workable.
items.cause: Too many coils added before the lower walls stiffened.
items.symptom: Walls bulge outward as coils accumulate

items.fix: Score the cracked surface, apply fresh slurry, press the coil back in position, and blend the interior seam.
items.cause: Insufficient scoring or slurry at the join.
items.symptom: A coil cracks away from the wall

attrs.heading: Common problems
